Dr. Harry Filippakis · UNE COM · Block 5

Mechanisms of Cancer Cell Growth & Metabolism

How tumor cells rewire glycolysis, glutamine, and signaling pathways to fuel uncontrolled proliferation — and how we exploit those vulnerabilities therapeutically. RAT Mode filters to Pre-Class PLOs.

Pre-Class PLOs3 PLOs
Class LOs3 LOs
Core ConceptWarburg Effect
Block5 — Endocrine/Metabolism
Metabolism Basics PLO 1 · Proliferation PLO 2 · Warburg PLO 3 · Mitochondria LO 1 · Oncogenes/TSG LO 2 · PI3K/mTOR/AMPK LO 3 · Therapy Summary

Foundation · Primer

Metabolism 101: Setting the Stage

Metabolism = all chemical processes required for life. Pathways consist of multiple enzyme-catalyzed steps, with rate-limiting enzymes that are tightly controlled at both the transcriptional level (e.g., mTOR → SREBP drives lipid synthesis genes) and post-translational level (phosphorylation, acetylation, methylation).

🏗️

Anabolic — Building Up

Consumes energy to create complex molecules from simple precursors.

  • Protein synthesis from amino acids
  • Lipid synthesis from fatty acid precursors
  • Polysaccharide synthesis from glucose
⚡

Catabolic — Breaking Down

Releases energy by degrading complex molecules into simple building blocks.

  • Glucose → CO₂ + H₂O (full oxidation)
  • Released energy stored in ATP

Slide 5 — Types of metabolic pathways — anabolism vs catabolism

🔑 ATP — The Metabolic Currency

Catabolism traps free energy in high-energy phosphate bonds of ATP. Enzyme-mediated hydrolysis releases that energy to drive anabolic reactions, muscle contraction, active transport, biosynthesis, and thermogenesis.

Slide 7 — ATP: the metabolic currency

Why this matters for cancer

A cell that commits to dividing has to double its biomass (proteins, lipids, nucleic acids, carbohydrates) and replicate its genome before cytokinesis. That's a massive biosynthetic load — and biosynthesis requires both building blocks (carbon skeletons) and reducing power (NADPH), not just ATP. Cancer cells evolve to solve this problem even when growth factors are absent.

Slide 8 — Metabolism in proliferating vs non-proliferating cells

Pre-Class PLO 1 — RAT Objective

Differentiate between metabolism in cancer vs. proliferating vs. non-proliferating cells.

Focus: nutrient fate (ATP vs biomass), dependence on growth factor signals, self-regulation mechanisms.

  • ✓
    Quiescent (non-proliferating) → Glucose + FA → TCA → ATP + CO₂. Goal = energy.
  • ✓
    Proliferating (normal) → GLUCOSE → BIOMASS + ATP + CO₂. Needs growth factor signal to commit.
  • ✓
    Cancer → Same biomass-generating profile as normal proliferating cells, but exaggerated and unresponsive to growth factor signals (growth-factor-independent).
  • ✓
    Self-regulation → Normal cells halt proliferation when nutrient supply exceeds demand. Cancer cells lose this brake.
  • ✓
    Two demands of tumor metabolism: (1) Biomass (protein, nucleic acid, lipid, carbs) and (2) Energy (ATP).
  • ✓
    Key fuel inputs for cancer: glucose AND glutamine (both increased uptake).
Pre-Class PLO 1

Cell-Type Metabolic Comparison

Cancer vs. Proliferating vs. Non-Proliferating Cells

All three cell populations run the same core metabolic pathways — what changes is the flux through each pathway and the fate of the carbon. The shift from energy-maximization to biomass-maximization is the defining feature of a proliferating cell.

The Three Metabolic States

STATE 1

Quiescent (Non-proliferating)

Goal: ATP for housekeeping.
Glucose + fatty acids → TCA cycle → ATP + CO₂. Mitochondria dominate. Growth factors absent, so no biosynthetic program runs.

STATE 2

Normal Proliferating

Goal: Build two daughter cells.
Glucose → biomass (nucleotides, lipids, proteins, carbohydrates) + ATP + CO₂. Requires growth factor signaling (e.g., embryonic dev, activated immune cells).

STATE 3

Cancer (Transformed)

Goal: Uncontrolled proliferation.
Same biomass output as State 2, but growth-factor independent. Oncogene/TSG mutations mimic constant growth-factor input — metabolism becomes constitutively "proliferative."

Slide 10 — Quiescent cell vs proliferating cell — nutrient fate

🧠 Concept — The Two Goals of Tumor Metabolism

Every tumor metabolism question eventually boils down to two competing needs: (1) Biomass (building blocks for new cells — protein, nucleic acid, lipid, carbs) and (2) Energy (ATP for ongoing work). Cancer cells sacrifice ATP-per-glucose efficiency to preserve carbon for biomass.

What "Self-Regulation" Means (and Why Cancer Loses It)

In normal tissue, when nutrient supply exceeds demand, proliferation halts. Growth-factor receptors normally gate the entire metabolic reprogramming — no signal, no nutrient uptake, no biosynthesis. Cancer cells bypass this checkpoint through:

  • Activating mutations in oncogenes (e.g., PI3K, KRAS, MYC) — constitutive "growth-factor-on" signal
  • Loss-of-function mutations in tumor suppressors (e.g., PTEN, LKB1, p53) — removal of metabolic brakes
  • AMPK insensitivity — the energy-stress sensor no longer shuts off anabolism when ATP drops

Cancer Cell Hallmark: Deregulating Cellular Energetics

Metabolic reprogramming is one of the core Hallmarks of Cancer (Hanahan & Weinberg). It's not independent — many proteins that drive the Warburg switch (MYC, HIF1α, AKT) are the same ones that drive proliferation, angiogenesis, and invasion.

Slide 12 — Hallmarks of Cancer — deregulating cellular energetics highlighted

Q: A resting hepatocyte and an embryonic stem cell both sit in a glucose-rich medium. The hepatocyte shuttles ~85% of its pyruvate into the mitochondrion; the stem cell shuttles much more pyruvate to lactate despite similar O₂. What best explains the stem cell's behavior?

Answer

The stem cell is actively proliferating under growth-factor signaling, so it diverts carbon away from full oxidation to preserve it for biomass synthesis (nucleotides via PPP, acetyl-CoA for lipids, amino acids). The hepatocyte is quiescent — its goal is pure ATP generation, so pyruvate enters the TCA cycle for maximum yield. Note: this is aerobic glycolysis in a normal proliferating cell, not the Warburg effect specifically, which requires cancer-level dysregulation.

Pre-Class PLO 2 — RAT Objective

Define aerobic glycolysis; the Warburg effect and its impact.

Focus: paradox of choosing inefficient glycolysis in presence of O₂, biosynthetic rationale, lactate's downstream effects.

  • ✓
    Warburg Effect = cancer cells use glycolysis → lactate even when O₂ is plentiful ("aerobic glycolysis"). ~4 mol ATP/mol glucose.
  • ✓
    OxPhos yields ~36 ATP/glucose; anaerobic glycolysis only 2 ATP/glucose. Warburg is the middle ground — and chosen by cancer.
  • ✓
    Why inefficient? To generate biosynthetic precursors: PPP → ribose-5-P → nucleotides, acetyl-CoA → lipids, NAD⁺ regeneration to sustain high glycolytic flux.
  • ✓
    Lactate is NOT waste. It creates an immune-permissive TME, polarizes macrophages to M2 (TAMs), activates NF-κB/PI3K in endothelium → metastasis, stabilizes HIF1α → VEGF → angiogenesis, drives fibroblasts to make hyaluronic acid → invasion.
  • ✓
    Clinical leverage: FDG-PET imaging exploits tumor glucose avidity — tumors light up; falling signal after therapy = responsive.
Pre-Class PLO 2

The Defining Metabolic Shift

Aerobic Glycolysis and the Warburg Effect

Energy Yields — Framing the Paradox

🫁

Oxidative Phosphorylation

~36 mol ATP / mol glucose

  • Requires O₂ + functional mitochondria
  • Glucose → pyruvate → acetyl-CoA → TCA → ETC
  • Default in quiescent differentiated tissue
⚠️

Anaerobic Glycolysis

~2 mol ATP / mol glucose

  • No O₂ required (or mitochondria absent)
  • Glucose → pyruvate → lactate
  • Default when O₂ is unavailable

Slide 15 — Oxidative phosphorylation — ~36 ATP/glucose in quiescent cells

Slide 16 — Anaerobic glycolysis — 2 ATP/glucose, lactate as end product

🔑 The Warburg Effect (Aerobic Glycolysis)

Cancer cells display increased rates of glycolysis despite the availability of O₂, sending ~85% of pyruvate to lactate even in fully oxygenated conditions. Yield is roughly ~4 mol ATP / mol glucose — a sweet spot higher than anaerobic glycolysis but vastly below OxPhos. Named after Otto Heinrich Warburg (Nobel Prize, 1931).

Slide 18 — Warburg effect — aerobic glycolysis across tumor types

Why Choose the Less Efficient Pathway?

The paradox: why would a rapidly dividing cell voluntarily take a 9× hit in ATP yield? The answer: ATP isn't the limiting resource — biomass is. Running glycolysis hot achieves three biosynthetic goals simultaneously:

PURPOSE 1

Nucleotide Synthesis

Glucose-6-P diverts into the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP), producing ribose-5-phosphate (sugar backbone of DNA/RNA) and NADPH (reducing power for biosynthesis and antioxidant defense).

PURPOSE 2

Acetyl-CoA for Lipogenesis

Glycolytic flux feeds mitochondrial citrate, which exits to cytosol via ACL → acetyl-CoA → malonyl-CoA → fatty acids. Membrane lipids for daughter cells.

PURPOSE 3

NAD⁺ Regeneration

Pyruvate → lactate (via LDH) regenerates NAD⁺, which is required to keep GAPDH running. Without NAD⁺ recycling, glycolysis would stall — so lactate production is what allows sustained glycolytic flux.

Slide 19 — Why cancer cells use a less efficient metabolic process

Lactate Is Not Metabolic Waste — It's a Signaling Molecule

Once regarded as a dead-end byproduct, lactate is now recognized as a potent modulator of the tumor microenvironment. The acidified, lactate-rich TME actively supports tumor progression on multiple fronts:

  1. Immune evasion → Lactate directly inhibits dendritic cell and cytotoxic T cell function, creating an immune-permissive microenvironment.
  2. Macrophage polarization → Lactate pushes tissue-resident macrophages toward the M2 state → tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) → immunosuppression.
  3. Endothelial activation → Lactate activates NF-κB and PI-3 kinase in endothelial cells → proinflammatory cytokines and matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) → facilitates metastasis.
  4. Angiogenesis → Lactate stabilizes HIF1α → tumor-stromal cells secrete VEGF → new blood vessels supply the tumor.
  5. Stromal remodeling → Lactate drives fibroblasts to produce hyaluronic acid → increased tumor invasiveness.

Slide 26 — Lactate drives 5 distinct pro-tumor effects in the microenvironment

🏥 Clinical Correlate — FDG-PET

Because tumors are glucose-avid, ¹⁸F-fluorodeoxyglucose PET is the workhorse imaging modality for cancer staging and treatment response. FDG is trapped inside cells after hexokinase phosphorylation. A patient with GI stromal tumor (GIST) on sunitinib (TKI) shows bright tumor uptake pre-therapy, complete extinction post-therapy — even when anatomic CT still shows a mass. Metabolic response precedes morphologic response.

⚠️ Boards-Favorite Distinction

Don't confuse anaerobic glycolysis (no O₂ available — e.g., exercising muscle, hypoxia) with aerobic glycolysis / Warburg (O₂ present but cell chooses to ferment anyway). Both end in lactate, but the drivers and cellular contexts are completely different. Warburg = paradoxical. Anaerobic = forced.

Q: A breast cancer cell line maintained at 21% O₂ produces ~85% of its pyruvate carbon as lactate, despite normal mitochondria and intact ETC. Pharmacologically blocking lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) causes rapid cessation of glycolysis. What is the mechanistic explanation?

Answer

Lactate production is required to regenerate NAD⁺, which is consumed by GAPDH at step 6 of glycolysis. Without NAD⁺ replenishment, GAPDH stalls, glycolysis halts, and the cell loses its source of biosynthetic precursors (ribose-5-P from PPP, acetyl-CoA from citrate export). In cancer cells operating at high glycolytic flux, mitochondrial NADH shuttles alone cannot keep up with NAD⁺ demand — so LDH is essential. This is also why LDH inhibitors are being explored as cancer therapeutics.

Pre-Class PLO 3 — RAT Objective

Describe the role of mitochondrial metabolism in cancer.

Focus: mitochondria are NOT shut down — they're repurposed as biosynthetic hubs. Glutaminolysis, truncated TCA, fatty acid oxidation for stress survival.

  • ✓
    Mitochondria are NOT dead in cancer — Warburg's original "mitochondrial damage" hypothesis is wrong. Tumors need mitochondria.
  • ✓
    TCA intermediates = biomass precursors: citrate → fatty acids/sterols, α-KG → amino acids, succinyl-CoA → heme, oxaloacetate → aspartate → nucleotides.
  • ✓
    Glutaminolysis: Glutamine → glutamate (GLS) → α-KG replenishes the TCA cycle when citrate is continually siphoned to cytosol for lipogenesis (anaplerosis). CB-839 is a clinical GLS inhibitor.
  • ✓
    Fatty acid oxidation is upregulated in some cancers during nutrient stress → acetyl-CoA → TCA → ATP. Metabolic flexibility = survival advantage.
  • ✓
    Druggable mito enzymes: GLS (CB-839), α-KG dehydrogenase (CPI-613). Mutant IDH1/IDH2 produce oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate.
Pre-Class PLO 3

The "Forgotten Organelle"

Mitochondrial Metabolism in Cancer

Warburg's original 1924 hypothesis was that cancer cells have damaged mitochondria and are forced into glycolysis. We now know this is wrong. Cancer cell mitochondria are functional — they've just been repurposed from ATP factories into biosynthetic hubs. Killing mitochondrial function still kills most tumors.

TCA Cycle Intermediates → Biomass

Every intermediate in the TCA cycle pulls double duty as a biosynthetic precursor. When a tumor cell is growing, carbon is continuously siphoned out of the cycle for anabolism. This means the cycle would collapse without replenishment — that's where glutamine comes in.

TCA IntermediateBiosynthetic DestinationProduct
CitrateExit via citrate transporter → cytosolic ACL → acetyl-CoAFatty acids, sterols, membrane lipids
α-KetoglutarateTransaminationGlutamate, amino acids, collagen hydroxylation
Succinyl-CoAALA synthase pathwayPorphyrins, heme
OxaloacetateTransamination → aspartatePurines, pyrimidines (nucleotide backbones)
Malate / OAAMalic enzyme / PEPCKNADPH (for biosynthesis), PEP → gluconeogenesis

Slide 27 — Mitochondrial TCA cycle as biosynthetic hub — CB-839 and CPI-613 drug targets shown

Glutamine — The Second Major Tumor Fuel

Because tumors constantly pull citrate out of the TCA cycle for lipogenesis, oxaloacetate would rapidly deplete. Tumors compensate via glutaminolysis:

Glutamine → (Glutaminase / GLS) → Glutamate → (GLUD1/GOT2/GPT2) → α-Ketoglutarate → TCA cycle replenishment

This anaplerotic reaction refills the TCA pool and sustains lipogenesis. MYC is a major driver of glutamine addiction — MYC transactivates glutamine transporters and GLS. CB-839 (telaglenastat) is a GLS inhibitor in clinical trials.

🧠 Concept — Anaplerosis vs. Cataplerosis

Anaplerosis = reactions that fill the TCA cycle (e.g., glutamine → α-KG, pyruvate → OAA via pyruvate carboxylase). Cataplerosis = reactions that drain it (e.g., citrate → cytosolic acetyl-CoA for lipids). In cancer, cataplerotic flux is enormous, so anaplerotic glutaminolysis becomes essential.

Fatty Acid Oxidation — The Stress-Survival Option

When glucose/glutamine are scarce (hypoxic tumor core, avascular niches, chemotherapy stress), some cancer cells flip on mitochondrial fatty acid β-oxidation (FAO). FAO generates acetyl-CoA that feeds the TCA cycle → ATP. This metabolic flexibility lets tumors survive transient nutrient deprivation — a key factor in therapy resistance and metastasis.

⚠️ Common Misconception

"Cancer cells turn off their mitochondria." False. They turn off the energy-maximizing function but upregulate the biosynthetic function. Mitochondrial TCA, FAO, and ETC remain active in most tumors — which is why drugs like metformin (complex I inhibitor) and CPI-613 (α-KG dehydrogenase/PDH inhibitor) work at all.

Oncometabolites — When Mutations Break the TCA

Mutations in IDH1 and IDH2 (isocitrate dehydrogenase) give neomorphic activity: they reduce α-ketoglutarate to 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG). 2-HG is an oncometabolite — it inhibits α-KG-dependent dioxygenases (histone demethylases, TET DNA demethylases) → epigenetic reprogramming. Classic findings: low-grade gliomas (IDH1), AML (IDH1/2). Detectable by 2-HG NMR imaging.

Other TCA-linked tumor syndromes: SDH (paragangliomas, pheo) and FH (hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell ca) — both accumulate succinate/fumarate, which also inhibit α-KG-dependent enzymes and stabilize HIF.

Q: A glioma shows an IDH1 mutation. Mass spec of tumor tissue reveals marked elevation of 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG). Which downstream mechanism best explains the tumorigenic effect of 2-HG?

Answer

2-HG structurally mimics α-ketoglutarate and competitively inhibits α-KG-dependent dioxygenases, including TET family DNA demethylases and JmjC histone demethylases. The result is a global hypermethylation phenotype (G-CIMP in glioma) that blocks differentiation and locks cells in a progenitor-like, proliferative state. Clinically detectable non-invasively by 2-HG magnetic resonance spectroscopy.

Class LO 1

Genetic Drivers of Metabolism

Oncogenes & Tumor Suppressor Genes in Metabolic Reprogramming

The metabolic phenotype of a cancer cell is encoded by its mutational landscape. Two general rules: oncogenes activate anabolism, and tumor suppressors restrain it. Loss of either set flips the cell into proliferation mode.

Key Oncogenes That Drive Metabolism

OncogeneMetabolic EffectClinical Relevance
MYC↑ glutamine transporters, ↑ GLS, ↑ nucleotide synthesis, ↑ ribosome biogenesisBurkitt lymphoma (t(8;14)), many solid tumors
HIF1α↑ GLUT1, ↑ glycolytic enzymes, ↑ LDH, ↑ VEGF (angiogenesis)Stabilized in hypoxia & by VHL loss (clear cell RCC)
RAS / RAF↑ glucose uptake, ↑ PI3K pathway, ↑ macropinocytosis (scavenging)Pancreatic, colon, lung adenocarcinomas
PI3K (PIK3CA)↑ glucose transport, ↑ lipogenesis (via AKT→SREBP), ↑ mTORBreast, endometrial, colon cancers
AKT↑ glucose uptake, ↑ hexokinase, ↑ mTORC1, ↑ cell survivalDownstream of PI3K — amplified in many cancers
FAS (FASN)Cytosolic palmitate synthesis → phospholipids, protein acylationOverexpressed in prostate, breast, ovarian ca; druggable

Key Tumor Suppressors That Restrain Metabolism

Tumor SuppressorNormal Metabolic FunctionLoss-of-Function Consequence
PTENLipid phosphatase — dephosphorylates PIP₃ → PIP₂ (opposes PI3K)Constitutive PI3K/AKT signaling; Cowden syndrome
LKB1 (STK11)Upstream activator of AMPK; also inhibits FASLoss → AMPK cannot restrain mTOR; Peutz-Jeghers; NSCLC
TSC1 / TSC2GAP for RHEB → inhibits mTORC1Loss → constitutive mTOR activity; tuberous sclerosis
p53↓ glycolysis (via TIGAR), ↑ OxPhos (SCO2), activates autophagyLoss → Warburg-favoring metabolism; Li-Fraumeni
VHLUbiquitin ligase — degrades HIF1α in normoxiaLoss → pseudohypoxic state; clear cell RCC

Neoplastic Lipogenesis — A Worked Example

Rapidly dividing tumors need enormous amounts of new lipid for membranes. Here's the integrated circuit that turns on:

  1. Warburg-high acetyl-CoA → mitochondrial citrate accumulates.
  2. Citrate exits the mitochondrion → cytosolic ACL (ATP-citrate lyase) → acetyl-CoA.
  3. Acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC) → malonyl-CoA.
  4. Fatty acid synthase (FAS/FASN) + NADPH (from PPP) → palmitate → long-chain fatty acids.
  5. Output: phospholipids for membranes, cholesterol esters, triglycerides, and lipid-based post-translational modifications (palmitoylation, myristoylation).

Slide 21 — Glycolytic switch feeding tumor lipogenesis — acetyl-CoA + NADPH converge on FAS

🧠 The LKB1–AMPK–ACC Axis

Normal cell: LKB1 (tumor suppressor) → phosphorylates/activates AMPK → phosphorylates/inactivates ACC → brake on fatty acid synthesis.

Cancer cell: LKB1 lost OR AMPK insensitive → ACC unchecked → runaway lipogenesis. Meanwhile, SREBP-1 (transcription factor, activated by mTOR) drives ACC/FAS transcription, amplifying the signal.

Class LO 2

Three Nodes Running the Show

PI3K, mTOR & AMPK in Cancer Cell Metabolism

These three signaling hubs translate nutrient availability and growth-factor signals into metabolic decisions. Understanding their wiring is the foundation for understanding most cancer-metabolism drugs.

1. PI3K–AKT — The "Grow and Survive" Signal

Receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) bound by insulin, IGF, or other growth factors recruit PI3K, which converts PIP₂ → PIP₃ at the plasma membrane. PIP₃ recruits AKT, which is phosphorylated by PDPK1 and mTORC2.

AKT outputs (all pro-tumor):

  • ↑ Glucose transporters (GLUT1 to membrane) and ↑ hexokinase activity → more glycolysis
  • ↑ Amino acid transport
  • ↑ Lipid/phospholipid synthesis
  • Activates mTORC1 (via TSC1/TSC2 inhibition → RHEB-GTP)
  • Inhibits FOXO (apoptosis factors), inhibits GSK3 → stabilizes SREBP, MYC, NRF2, HIF1
  • Tagline: AKT = cell survival

Tumor suppressor opposing PI3K: PTEN — dephosphorylates PIP₃ back to PIP₂. PTEN loss = constitutive AKT activation (Cowden syndrome; common in endometrial, prostate, glioblastoma).

Slide 22 — PI3K–AKT signaling — nodes of cancer-associated mutations

2. mTOR — The Growth Integrator

Mechanistic Target Of Rapamycin. mTORC1 (raptor-containing) integrates four inputs: growth factors (via PI3K/AKT), amino acids (especially leucine via Rag GTPases), energy status (inhibited by AMPK), and oxygen (inhibited under hypoxia).

mTORC1 activation outputs:

  • ↑ Protein synthesis (phosphorylates S6K, inhibits 4E-BP1 → releases eIF4F for cap-dependent translation)
  • ↑ Aerobic glycolysis (via HIF1α)
  • ↑ Lipid synthesis (via SREBP1)
  • ↑ Nucleotide synthesis (via CAD)
  • ↑ Pentose phosphate pathway (via G6PD)
  • Inhibits autophagy (via ULK1, TFEB)
💊 Drug Class — mTOR Inhibitors

Sirolimus (rapamycin) and everolimus are allosteric mTORC1 inhibitors that bind FKBP12. Clinically used in: renal cell carcinoma (everolimus), pancreatic NETs, breast cancer (with exemestane), tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC1/TSC2 loss → unopposed mTOR — a tumor syndrome Dr. Filippakis studies).

Slide 23 — mTOR integrates growth signals — sirolimus/everolimus block mTORC1

3. AMPK — The Energy Sensor (and Master Brake)

AMP-activated protein kinase senses the AMP:ATP ratio. When ATP falls (nutrient deprivation, hypoxia, metformin), AMP rises and AMPK is phosphorylated by its upstream activator LKB1 (tumor suppressor!).

Activated AMPK — normal function = inhibit anabolism, activate catabolism:

  • Phosphorylates/inhibits ACC → ↓ fatty acid synthesis, ↑ FA oxidation
  • Phosphorylates TSC2 → inhibits mTORC1 → ↓ protein synthesis
  • Phosphorylates p53 → cell cycle arrest and ↓ glycolysis
  • Activates ULK1 → autophagy (recycling nutrients)
⚠️ Key Cancer Concept

In cancer, AMPK becomes insensitive to energy status (often via LKB1 loss, or via constitutive mTOR/AKT signaling overriding AMPK). The metabolic brake is disabled — anabolism runs unchecked even when ATP is low. This is why metformin (which activates AMPK indirectly by inhibiting mitochondrial complex I) is under investigation as an adjunct cancer therapy.

Slide 24 — AMPK signaling — master switch inhibiting anabolism, activating catabolism

Putting It All Together

Growth factor → RTK → PI3K → PIP₃ → AKT → mTORC1 → ↑ protein/lipid/nucleotide synthesis, ↑ glycolysis
↑ AMP:ATP → LKB1 → AMPK → ⊣ mTORC1, ⊣ ACC → ↓ anabolism
Cancer = hyperactive PI3K/AKT/mTOR and silenced AMPK brake

Q: A patient with tuberous sclerosis complex (germline TSC2 loss) develops renal angiomyolipomas. Which drug class is expected to reduce tumor size by targeting the underlying molecular defect?

Answer

mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus). Loss of TSC2 removes the GAP activity on RHEB → RHEB-GTP persistently activates mTORC1 → runaway protein synthesis, glycolysis, and lipogenesis. Everolimus is FDA-approved for TSC-associated renal angiomyolipomas and subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGAs). This is a textbook "targeted therapy matching the germline mutation" scenario.

Class LO 3

Clinical Translation

Therapeutic Targeting of Cancer Metabolism

Every metabolic vulnerability outlined above is in some stage of drug development. The therapeutic landscape falls into four broad strategies, paired with metabolic imaging for patient stratification and response monitoring.

Imaging — FDG-PET as a Metabolic Biomarker

¹⁸F-FDG (a glucose analog) is taken up by GLUT transporters and trapped after hexokinase phosphorylation (FDG-6-P cannot proceed further). Tumors with high glycolytic flux glow bright on PET. Critically, metabolic response precedes anatomic shrinkage — a tumor may still appear on CT but show no FDG uptake if therapy is working.

Slide 29 — FDG-PET pre- and post-sunitinib in GIST — metabolic response without anatomic change

Four Strategies for Attacking Tumor Metabolism

STRATEGY 1

Deplete Extrinsic Nutrients

Reduce what tumor can scavenge. Examples: glucose restriction / ketogenic diet, L-asparaginase (depletes asparagine in ALL), methionine restriction.

STRATEGY 2

Block Nutrient Uptake

Inhibit GLUT transporters, CD36/FABP fatty acid uptake, glutamine transporters. Starves the tumor directly.

STRATEGY 3

Inhibit Endogenous Biosynthesis

Block lipid (FAS inhibitors), NAD⁺ (NAMPT inhibitors), adenine, methionine, or nucleotide biosynthesis from within. Many antimetabolites (methotrexate, 5-FU) already do this.

STRATEGY 4

Promote Toxic Metabolite Build-up

Create metabolic imbalances (e.g., SCD1 inhibition → saturated FA accumulation → ER stress → tumor death). Exploits lipotoxicity.

Specific Drug Targets Covered

TargetDrug / StrategyMechanism
Mitochondrial Complex IMetforminIndirect AMPK activation → ↓ mTOR, ↓ gluconeogenesis. Under trial in cancer.
Hexokinase / Glycolysis2-DG, 3-bromopyruvate (investigational)Blocks first committed step of glycolysis
Glutaminase (GLS)CB-839 (telaglenastat)Blocks glutamine → glutamate → α-KG; starves MYC-driven tumors
α-KG dehydrogenase / PDHCPI-613 (devimistat)TCA cycle inhibitor; trialed in pancreatic ca
mTORC1Sirolimus, EverolimusAllosteric FKBP12-binding inhibitors. Approved in RCC, SEGA, AML
FAS (FASN)TVB-2640 (investigational)Blocks palmitate synthesis → starves membrane biogenesis
PI3KAlpelisib (PIK3CA-selective)Approved for HR+/HER2− breast cancer with PIK3CA mutations
AKTCapivasertib, ipatasertibNewer approvals in breast cancer
Mutant IDH1/IDH2Ivosidenib, EnasidenibDirectly blocks 2-HG production; approved in AML, cholangiocarcinoma

Slide 30 — Four therapeutic strategies targeting tumor metabolic vulnerabilities

The Reality — Tumor Heterogeneity

A single solid tumor is not metabolically uniform. Hypoxic cores use different fuels than well-vascularized edges. Cancer stem cells often rely on OxPhos while bulk tumor cells run Warburg. This metabolic heterogeneity explains why single-agent metabolic drugs often fail — combination therapies (e.g., metabolic inhibitor + immune checkpoint blockade + targeted therapy) are the direction of the field.

Designing a Precision Metabolic Regimen

  1. Profile the tumor genetically (DNA/RNA seq, expression panels like MammaPrint) to identify driver mutations that predict metabolic dependencies.
  2. Image metabolically with FDG-PET (glycolysis) or 2-HG NMR (IDH mutants) to confirm functional phenotype.
  3. Pair targeted metabolic inhibitors (CB-839, CPI-613, mTORi, IDH-inhibitors) with combination therapies — checkpoint blockade (anti-PD1), other TKIs, or chemo/radiation.
  4. Monitor response with serial FDG-PET — metabolic decline often precedes anatomic regression.

Slide 31 — Designing metabolic cancer therapies — integrating genetics, imaging, and combination regimens

Q: A patient with hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer has a tumor biopsy showing a PIK3CA hotspot mutation (H1047R). Which targeted agent is most appropriate to add to her endocrine therapy based on the molecular profile?

Answer

Alpelisib, a PI3K-α-selective inhibitor, is FDA-approved (2019) for HR+/HER2− advanced breast cancer with PIK3CA mutations, used in combination with fulvestrant. The SOLAR-1 trial demonstrated improved progression-free survival. Rationale: PIK3CA mutation drives constitutive PI3K→AKT→mTOR activation, which upregulates the glycolytic and lipogenic programs that fuel growth. Notable toxicity: hyperglycemia (on-target — PI3K is also critical for insulin signaling).

Bringing It Together

High-Yield Summary

🔑 The Six Takeaways

1. Signaling pathways and metabolism are integrated, not parallel — every growth-factor signal has a metabolic consequence.

2. Increased aerobic glycolysis (Warburg) is essential for cancer cell survival — it provides biomass precursors, not just ATP.

3. Oncogene gain-of-function + tumor-suppressor loss-of-function → cells become unresponsive to growth-factor signals → constitutive proliferative and metabolic programs.

4. Core metabolic changes: aerobic glycolysis, glutaminolysis, lipogenesis.

5. The key regulators:
• AMPK — normal brake on anabolism; silenced in cancer (often via LKB1 loss)
• PI3K/AKT — most commonly mutated pathway; opposed by PTEN; drives mTOR and nutrient uptake
• mTOR — activated by PI3K/AKT, inhibited by AMPK; increases cell growth by releasing eIF4F (via 4E-BP1 phosphorylation)

6. Clinical applications: dietary interventions (calorie/glucose restriction), FDG-PET to monitor response, and a growing suite of targeted metabolic drugs (metformin, alpelisib, CB-839, mTOR inhibitors, IDH inhibitors).

Slide 32 — Summary slide — cross-talk between signaling and metabolism

🧠 One-Sentence Synthesis

Cancer is a disease of uncoupled metabolic control: proliferative signaling runs without growth-factor permission, the AMPK energy-brake is silenced, and the cell commits to a biomass-maximizing metabolic program (Warburg + glutaminolysis + lipogenesis) that produces the biomolecular raw material for continual division — and which we can exploit therapeutically by targeting the uptake, the biosynthesis, or the pathway that orchestrates them.
